Definition and historical development of biosensors; overview of biosensor components: biorecognition elements, transducers, signal processors; applications of biosensors

Biorecognition Elements: enzymes - catalysis, specificity, enzyme-based biosensors; antibodies - antigen-antibody interactions, immunosensors; nucleic acids - DNA/RNA hybridization, aptamers; cells and tissues - whole-cell biosensors, tissue-based biosensors
Immobilization Techniques: physical adsorption, covalent bonding, cross-linking, entrapment; advantages and limitations of each immobilization method
Optical biosensors: fluorescence, surface plasmon resonance, bioluminescence
Piezoelectric biosensors: quartz crystal microbalance, surface acoustic wave devices
Types and classification of transducers: primary, secondary, active, and passive
Mechanical Transducers: strain gauges, piezoelectric transducer
Magnetic Transducers: Hall effect transducers, LVDT
Transducer Characteristics: Static Characteristics - accuracy, precision, resolution, sensitivity, linearity
Transducer Characteristics: Dynamic Characteristics - response time, frequency response, bandwidth
Nanotechnology: use of nanomaterials (nanoparticles, nanotubes, nanowires) in biosensors, nanobiosensors
Lab-on-a-Chip: integration of biosensing and microfluidics, advantages of miniaturization
Implantable sensors and wearable health sensors
Project Based Learning: literature review and presentation on a selected biosensor application (e.g., glucose monitoring, detection of pollutants in water)
Project Based Learning: selection of a specific type of transducer for the given application
Project Based Learning: analysis and presentation of working principles, characteristics, and application considerations of the selected transducer
Project Based Learning: design, development, and testing of a comprehensive biosensor system based on knowledge and skills acquired throughout the course
Project Based Learning: team work to create a fully functional biosensor, document the process, and present the project to the class
Project Based Learning: two evaluations - after one month of commencement of classes and at the end of the course
Difference Between Microprocessor and Microcontroller Architecture, 8051 Pin Diagram

Architecture, I/O Port Structure, Register Organization - Special Function Registers, Memory Organization 8051 Microcontrollers: Instruction Set, Addressing Modes, Simple Assembly Language Programs: Arithmetic (Addition, Subtraction, Multiplication and Division), Transfer a Block of Data from One Internal Memory Location to Another
8051 Microcontroller Programming and Peripherals, Timers/Counters, Serial Communication, Interfacing of Peripherals (ALP and Embedded C Programming), LCD

Definition, Features, Simple Example of Embedded Systems, Applications of Embedded Systems - Consumer Electronics, Robotics, Automobiles, Embedded System Architecture: HW - Processor, Controller, SoC, Memory, Peripherals

SW - Application, Middleware, OS, Device Drivers, Tool Chain - Assembler, Interpreter, Compiler, Linker, Loader, Debugger, Embedded System Design Process: Requirement Analysis, Specification Development, HW and SW Co-Design and Development, Module Integration, Testing
Board Study (Board Level Block Schematic) - Chip (Features Only - Architecture Not Needed)

Temperature Monitoring System Using LM35 Temperature Sensor and Seven Segment Display, Suggestion on Project Topics, The Objective of Project Based Learning is to Strengthen the Understanding of Student's Fundamentals Through Effective Application of Theoretical Concepts, Projects Can Help to Boost Student's Skills and Widen the Horizon of Their Thinking, The Ultimate Aim of an Engineering Student is to Resolve a Problem by Applying Theoretical Knowledge, In the First Class, Students Shall be Given an Awareness and/or Guideline on How to Implement and Learn This Course Through a Project and Form Groups, Each Group Should Identify a Project Relevant to the Subject of Study, Develop the Block Diagram and Present Before the Jury Within First Month After the Commencement of Classes, Implement Necessary Hardware and Software to Develop the Prototype, Prepare a Report and Present Before the Jury for Final Evaluation

External and Serial Interrupts, Programming to Handle All Types of Interrupts in Assembly Language

Introduction to PIC Microcontrollers: Architectural Features, Core and Peripheral Features

Project Based Learning: In the first class, students shall be given an awareness and/or guidelines on how to implement and learn this course through a project and form groups of maximum 5 members in the first week. Each group identify a project where microcontrollers can be used for simple biomedical applications and perform a literature survey. Choose a suitable microcontroller/microcontroller-based development boards for your application and the input-output devices to be interfaced. Develop the block diagram and present before the jury within first month after the commencement of classes. Implement necessary hardware and software to develop the prototype. Prepare a report and present before the jury for final evaluation.

Applications of Operational Amplifiers: Basic applications, Inverting amplifier, Non-inverting amplifier, Differential amplifier, Scale changer, Summing amplifier, Subtractor, Adder, Voltage follower, V-I converter (grounded load type and floating load type), Howland constant current circuit and I-V converter

Introduction to Signals, Systems and Digital Signal Processing: Continuous and Discrete Time Signals, Sampling, Elementary Discrete Time Signals, Classification of signals, Operations on Signals, Discrete Time Systems, LTI Systems, Convolution sum, Difference Equation representation, Z-transform, and Basic Elements of DSP System
Frequency Domain Representation of Discrete-Time Signals: DTFT, DFT and its properties, Circular convolution, FFT Algorithms (Radix-2 DIT and DIF), and IDFT computation using Radix-2 FFT Algorithms

Design of Digital Filters: Classification of FIR and IIR Filters, Design of linear phase FIR filters using Window method (Rectangular, Hamming, Hanning), Design of IIR Digital Filters using Butterworth prototype, Impulse Invariance and Bilinear Transformation methods, and Frequency Transformations in the Analog domain

Realization of Digital Filters: Block diagram and signal flow graph representations, FIR Filter Structures (Linear Phase, Direct Form, Cascade Form), IIR Filter Structures (Direct Form, Cascade Form, Parallel Form), DSP architecture of TMS320C67xx, and Finite word length effects in DSP systems

Introduction to formal language theory - Alphabets, Strings, Concatenation, Languages, Regular Languages - Deterministic Finite State Automata (DFA), Nondeterministic Finite State Automata (NFA), Equivalence of DFA and NFA, Regular Grammar, and Equivalence of RGs and DFA

Regular Expression (RE), Equivalence of REs and DFA, Pumping Lemma for regular languages, Closure Properties of Regular Languages, DFA state minimization (Myhill-Nerode Theorem), and Applications of MNT

Context Free Grammar (CFG), derivation trees and ambiguity, Nondeterministic Pushdown Automata (PDA), Deterministic Pushdown Automata (DPDA), Pumping Lemma for Context-Free Languages, and Closure Properties of Context Free Languages

Context Sensitive Languages - Context Sensitive Grammar (CSG), Linear Bounded Automata, Turing Machines - Standard Turing Machine, Robustness, Recursive and Recursively Enumerable Languages, and Chomsky classification of formal languages

8051 Microcontroller: Introduction, comparison of Microprocessor and microcontroller, Evolution of microcontrollers from 4-bit to 32-bit, Selection of microcontrollers, Applications of microcontrollers, 8051 Architecture - Registers, Pin diagram, I/O ports functions, Internal Memory organization, and External Memory (ROM & RAM) interfacing

Addressing modes and accessing memory using various addressing modes, Instruction set: Arithmetic, Logical, Simple bit, jump, loop and call instructions, Stack, Stack and Subroutine instructions, and Assembly language program examples

Timers and Counters, Interrupts, interfacing seven segment displays, displaying information on an LCD, control of a stepper Motor, DAC, 8051 Serial Communication - Basics of Serial Data Communication, RS-232 standard, 9 pin RS232 signals, and Simple Serial Port programming to transmit and receive data
